title: Fix: XC live-TV placeholder so Dispatcharr VOD sync actually triggers
description: Root cause of the long-standing 'No streams returned from Xtream Codes provider' bug (blocked VOD groups from loading in Dispatcharr): Dispatcharr's XC-account refresh always fetches live categories/streams first (get_live_categories/get_all_live_streams) and only queues the separate VOD-refresh task (apps.vod.tasks.refresh_vod_content) as the LAST statement in that same function -- unreachable if the live-stream check aborts early. xc_server.py never implemented get_live_categories/get_live_streams (VOD Manager is VOD-only), so Dispatcharr always saw zero live streams, hit its own safety abort ('No streams collected... aborting refresh'), set the account to ERROR, and returned before ever queuing the VOD refresh -- meaning VOD content never synced for any account. Fixed by adding a single clearly-fake placeholder live category+stream in xc_server.py, just enough to satisfy Dispatcharr's non-empty check. Verified end-to-end against a real Dispatcharr instance (dispatcharr-test): after the fix, the live-refresh succeeds, the VOD-refresh task is actually queued, and it synced the full real catalog (56506 movies, 5599 series) successfully. Root-caused by reading Dispatcharr's actual source (apps/m3u/tasks.py, apps/vod/tasks.py) from both a local test image and GitHub main.
close_reason: Implemented in xc_server.py (get_live_categories/get_live_streams placeholder). Verified end-to-end against real dispatcharr-test: live-refresh no longer aborts, VOD-refresh task actually gets queued, full real catalog (56506 movies, 5599 series) synced successfully. Not yet committed/pushed; real-world verification against the actual remote affected instance (dispatch3, account 26) still pending until this is deployed there.

title: Fix blank-provider-name import collision + add manual rename capability
description: User found: FTV sent 3 completely unrelated movies (different genres: Science Fiction, Spanish Movies, Romance) with blank/empty names, which silently collapsed into ONE pool entry because bulk_import_movies matched on exact (name='', year=NULL) -- an empty string is not a real identity but was treated like one. Fixed: blank names never match anything (including each other) at import time; each gets its own row with a distinguishing placeholder name (category + stream id) and gets flagged needs_year_review=1, with re-sync reuse via provider+stream_id lookup so it doesn't mint a fresh orphaned row every catalog refresh. Applied the same fix to bulk_import_series (no series instances found, but same latent bug). Split the one already-corrupted movie (id=70587) back into 3 separate rows. Also: there was NO way to manually correct a movie/series' own name/year at all -- added rename_item() (same merge-on-collision safety as resolve_missing_artwork/resolve_year_review) plus a 'Rename / fix year' control in both MovieRow and SeriesRow's detail view.
close_reason: Fixed and deployed. Verified live via Playwright: split entries (id=70587 '[Untitled] Science Fiction · stream 200584', new ids 270149/270150 for the other two sources) all correctly show as distinct movies with 1 source each, flagged in Needs Review (3). Tested the new rename UI end-to-end: opened the detail view, clicked 'Rename / fix year', changed the name, saved, confirmed the DB updated correctly and needs_year_review cleared, then reverted the test value and restored the review flag so the real placeholder is still there for the user's own manual pass. No other blank-name instances existed elsewhere in the pool (checked both movies and series tables).

title: Security hardening: password log leaks, login lockout, weak password hashing
description:
Follow-up from a security review the user asked for ('any issues for a potential public-facing product?'). Four confirmed/fixed issues:
1. Plaintext XC client passwords in logs (CONFIRMED via actual observed log output) -- xc_server.py's _log_hit logged request.url.path unredacted; the XC protocol embeds password as a URL PATH segment (/username/password/...), not just query string, so the existing query-param-only redaction missed it across 11 route call sites (every stream/preview request). ALSO found uvicorn's own access logger had the same gap separately (different logger, main.py's _RedactPasswordFilter only handled password= query-string style).
2. No brute-force protection on the admin login (/api/auth/login/) -- unlike XC client login which already had configurable lockout.
3. Password hashing used a single-round salted SHA-256, not a proper password-hashing KDF -- fast enough to be crackable offline at scale if the config file ever leaked.
4. require_auth silently no-ops when no credentials are configured yet, and the first-run screen has a Skip button -- an instance can run fully unauthenticated indefinitely without the operator realizing it.
close_reason:
All fixed, deployed, and verified live:
1. Password log leaks: fixed BOTH the app's own xc_server.py logger (_log_hit now masks the exact password value from the resolved path param across all 10 affected routes) AND uvicorn's separate access logger (main.py's _RedactPasswordFilter extended with a regex matching the vm-XXXXXXXX username format to mask the following path segment). Verified via a real request: both log lines now show /preview/movie-source/vm-a189ccb0/***/1.mp4 instead of the real password.
2. Admin login lockout: added a per-IP tracker (8 attempts / 5min window / 15min lockout, mirrors the existing XC lockout shape) to /api/auth/login/. Verified live: 8th consecutive failed attempt returned 429 instead of 401.
3. Password hashing upgraded to PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256 (260k iterations, OWASP 2023 minimum) with a scheme marker for backward compat -- existing legacy SHA-256 accounts are transparently upgraded on next successful login, no forced password reset. Verified in isolation (not touching the real account): wrong password rejected without migrating, correct legacy password accepted and upgraded, subsequent logins work via the new scheme.
4. No-credentials-configured state now logs a clear, hard-to-miss startup warning so an operator running unauthenticated (via the first-run Skip button) sees it in their own logs rather than it being silently invisible. Left the Skip button itself in place since some deployments are genuinely LAN/VPN-only and don't want a forced login.

title: Automated instance connect set Dispatcharr M3U account max_streams=1, blocking all but one concurrent stream
description: Discovered live 2026-07-21: user reported a series that showed as playing in VOD Manager Activity but never actually played in the client app (UHF); later movies stopped playing entirely too. Root-caused via Dispatcharr's own logs (its VOD proxy is verbose enough to show real client user-agent and profile capacity per request): connect_dispatcharr_instance in vod_sync.py hardcoded max_streams=1 on the Dispatcharr M3U account it creates -- an account-level cap on total concurrent connections Dispatcharr will pull from VOD Manager, separate from and layered above the real per-provider limits (which ARE correctly enforced via the per-provider profiles). With the cap at 1, any second near-simultaneous connection attempt (a player's ordinary probe-then-play double-connect, which is exactly what happened for the Batman episode -- two requests 10s apart, second one hit 'Profile ... connection limit exceeded on session reuse') got hard-rejected. The rejected/failed downstream leg looked from VOD Manager's own side like nothing unusual, since VOD Manager only sees its own upstream relay connection to the real provider, which succeeded and ran to completion -- explaining exactly the reported symptom of VOD Manager showing something as actively playing while the client showed nothing.
close_reason: Fixed immediately (live, unblocking playback right away) by PATCHing max_streams from 1 to 50 on both existing Dispatcharr M3U accounts (dispatch-test id=27, dispatch2 id=23) directly via their own APIs. Fixed at the code level by replacing the hardcoded max_streams: 1 in vod_sync.py's connect_dispatcharr_instance with a named _DEFAULT_ACCOUNT_MAX_STREAMS=50 constant, with a comment explaining why this needs to be generous (the real enforcement happens per-provider via profiles, this is just a top-of-funnel cap that should never itself be the bottleneck) -- so any future 'Connect a new instance' automation run creates the account with a sane default instead of reintroducing this bug. README.md's 'Connecting Dispatcharr instances' section now mentions the 50-stream default and why. Rebuilt and restarted the container. 50 was chosen as generously above any realistic single-household concurrent-VOD-stream count while still being a real (non-infinite) safety cap; the real per-provider ceilings already known and enforced (Infinity=20, 4KLive=18, etc.) remain the actual meaningful limits.

title: Harden XC catalog import: year type coercion + per-row batch resilience
description: Two related hardening fixes prompted by an analogous bug found in a parallel project (EDM tool): a provider sending series year as a string crashed there under Postgres (SQLite silently tolerated the same class of bug here). Fixed: (1) vod_importer.py's series import now coerces the provider's raw 'year' field via a new _coerce_year() helper instead of passing it through uncast -- SQLite's INTEGER column affinity was masking this, but malformed values (empty string, 'N/A', a date string) would still corrupt the year column and break the (name,year) exact-match dedup used everywhere including the Duplicate Finder. (2) vod_db.py's four bulk_import_* functions had no per-item error isolation -- one bad row (missing key, unexpected type) would raise and either lose the ENTIRE batch (bulk_import_movies/series only commit once, at the end) or abandon the rest of a large batch partway through. Added a shared _item_savepoint() context manager (SAVEPOINT/ROLLBACK TO/RELEASE) around each item, plus a nested savepoint per-episode in bulk_import_plex_series so one bad episode doesn't roll back its whole series. Also fixed a counter-drift bug found via testing: created/matched/flagged counts were incrementing before the item's last statement ran, drifting out of sync with what actually landed in the DB when that statement raised -- deferred via local did_create/did_match/did_flag flags folded into the real counters only after full success. Verified with real sqlite functional tests for all 4 functions including the nested-episode case.
close_reason: Implemented and verified with real sqlite functional tests: year coercion (vod_importer._coerce_year) and per-item savepoint isolation across all 4 bulk_import_* functions in vod_db.py, including nested per-episode savepoints and corrected created/matched/flagged counter accuracy. Not yet committed/pushed.

title: Independent code review pass: 7 findings from session's language-filter/security work
description: Spawned an independent code-review agent (no prior context) over the full session diff (9203ecf..HEAD, ~2279 lines). Found and fixed: (1) blank-name import-collision fix was applied to bulk_import_movies/series but not the parallel bulk_import_plex_movies/series -- same corruption class still reachable via Plex; (2) the new admin-login lockout tracker had no periodic sweep (unlike xc_server's, which it explicitly mirrors) -- unbounded memory growth from every distinct IP that ever fails a login once; (3) missing-artwork bulk-exclude only ran the sibling-protection check when a script/prefix filter was active, reopening the exact unprotected-archive bug already fixed for library-language; (4) GET /missing-artwork/ and /library-language/ each called list+count separately, both re-running the same expensive full-table Python scan -- and library-language's scan ran unconditionally even with NO language filter active, fetching all 244k+ rows on every page load; (5) minor: xc_server's password log-redaction did a global str.replace on an attacker-controlled (pre-auth) value, which could corrupt unrelated path segments; (6) Duplicate Finder's merge mutation had no onError handler and didn't refetch its (enabled:false) query on success, so merged groups kept showing stale; (7) resolve_missing_artwork's collision-merge path splits work across two connections/commits -- accepted as a low-probability, easily-recoverable, already-established pattern (matches resolve_year_review), not fixed.
close_reason: 6 of 7 fixed and deployed, verified: (1) Plex import blank-name guard mirrors the XC fix exactly, compile-checked; (2) login lockout sweep added, mirrors xc_server's pattern; (3) missing-artwork exclude now always smart-checks (verified live: 50-item real sample -> 9 archived, 41 correctly skipped); (4) combined list_missing_artwork_page/list_library_page verified byte-identical output to the old two-call approach with real measured speedup (missing-artwork ~2x faster; library-language fast-path unconditional full-scan eliminated entirely -- was fetching all 244k+ rows on every page load with no filter active at all); (5) precise segment-based password redaction replacing the global str.replace, verified live in logs; (6) added onError + explicit refetch() (invalidateQueries alone doesn't refetch an enabled:false query) to Duplicate Finder's merge mutation. (7) left as accepted low-risk, matches an existing established pattern elsewhere (resolve_year_review) -- not worth a bigger refactor for a process-crash-mid-function edge case.

description: User correctly caught that changing 'keep a title if also available as' had no visible effect on the Archive button's displayed count -- it always showed the raw filtered total, not what smart_bulk_exclude would actually do, making the field look non-functional even though it worked correctly under the hood. Added dry_run support to smart_bulk_exclude and both bulk-exclude routes (missing-artwork and library-language), plus a live preview query in both modals so the button text updates to 'Archive N of M -- K would be skipped' as filters/keep-codes change. Independently re-verified the underlying logic itself was already correct via a full manual trace (24-item apex test batch, exact match between hand-computed and actual output) before concluding the ONLY issue was the missing UI feedback.

title: Whole-library Language Filter modal with smart sibling-aware archiving
description: Extends the language-prefix/non-Latin-script filters from Missing Artwork to the ENTIRE movie/series pool (not just items missing a poster) via a new 'Language Filter' modal reachable from both Movies and TV Shows tabs. Archiving here always runs vod_db.smart_bulk_exclude: a title is only archived if a same-content sibling (bare title with the language prefix stripped) also exists in a kept language (a user-picked code, or unprefixed/no-tag) -- otherwise it's skipped and reported, so a deployment never loses its only copy of something just because it's not in a preferred language. keep_codes threaded into Missing Artwork's own archive flow too for consistency.
close_reason: Built, deployed, verified via Playwright + DB checks. Found and fixed a real bug during live testing: the library-language bulk-exclude route only ran the smart sibling check when body.script or prefix_list was truthy -- a plain free-text search (no prefix chip / script checkbox) fell through to the unconditional bulk_set_review_excluded, archiving 540 Arabic documentary titles with zero sibling protection. Fixed to always run smart_bulk_exclude for this modal's archive action regardless of which filter dimension produced the candidate set (un-archive still applies directly, never destructive). Re-verified: same search now correctly archives 49 (had a same-content sibling) and skips 493 (no alternative-language copy, reported by name). All test archives reverted afterward to leave the real catalog untouched.

title: Duplicate Finder: scan/merge same-year punctuation-variant duplicates

Built a Curation & Maintenance scan+review+merge tool (vod_db.find_duplicate_groups/merge_duplicate_group) with the same trust pattern as Orphan Checker -- review before merge, not automatic.

Also paginated the results client-side (20/page) after discovering 4,322 groups rendered flat made the page unusably slow -- confirmed via Playwright click timeouts before the fix, clean after.

title: XC catalog responses never included poster artwork, cast, or director
description: Discovered live 2026-07-20 via real UHF testing: get_vod_streams/get_vod_info/get_series/get_series_info all hardcoded stream_icon/cover/cover_big/cast/director to empty strings, even though movies.poster_url/cast_list/director and series.poster_url/cast_list/director are real, populated columns (captured during enrichment). Client apps (UHF specifically flagged this as blank artwork) had nothing to display even for fully-enriched content.
close_reason: Fixed: added poster_url/cast_list/director to the four export-row queries in vod_db.py (get_movie_export_rows, get_movie_export_row_by_stream_id, get_series_export_rows, get_series_export_row_by_export_id) and wired them into xc_server.py's get_vod_streams (stream_icon), get_vod_info (cover_big/cast/director), get_series (cover), get_series_info (cover/cast/director) -- replacing hardcoded empty strings. Verified live against the real production pool: 56,368/56,506 movie streams now return a real TMDB poster URL, 5,620/5,620 exposed series return cover art, and a spot-checked movie's get_vod_info correctly returns real cast and director strings alongside its poster. Coverage matches enrichment state (unenriched items still return empty, as expected -- nothing to show yet, not a bug) and will grow automatically as background enrichment continues.

title: XC server froze under a real Dispatcharr full-catalog VOD sync
description: Discovered live 2026-07-20 during real dispatch2 testing: UHF could see all VOD Manager categories via Dispatcharr but no video would play. Root cause turned out to be upstream of that -- while Dispatcharr was doing its initial full VOD catalog sync (processing_groups), the whole VOD Manager server froze completely (confirmed: even a trivial /api/auth/verify/ request timed out for 15+ seconds, process stuck in CPU R state) until manually restarted. Root cause: xc_server.py's player_api.php action handlers and _authenticate ran fully synchronous vod_db (SQLite) calls directly inside async route handlers without asyncio.to_thread, so any slow synchronous work blocked FastAPI's single event-loop thread for every other concurrent request. get_series_info specifically had a real N+1 query pattern (list_episodes then one separate get_episode_export_row query per episode, each opening its own SQLite connection) -- for a long-running show (hundreds to thousands of episodes, e.g. Survivor at 719 or EastEnders at 3320) that is genuinely hundreds/thousands of synchronous connection-open-query-close cycles in one blocking call, and Dispatcharr's full sync hits get_series_info for every series in the account in a tight burst.
close_reason: Fixed with two changes in xc_server.py + vod_db.py: (1) new vod_db.get_episode_export_rows_for_series() replaces the N+1 per-episode loop in get_series_info with a single bulk query (one SQLite round-trip instead of up to thousands). (2) player_api.php's entire action-dispatch body was extracted into a plain sync function (_handle_player_api_action) and run via asyncio.to_thread instead of inline in the async route handler; _authenticate's client-matching loop and record_xc_client_seen write were similarly moved off the event loop via asyncio.to_thread. Together these mean one client's heavy request (or a burst of them, like a full Dispatcharr catalog sync) can no longer block every other concurrent request. Verified live: fired a concurrent burst of 15 get_series_info requests against real large series (up to 719 episodes, Survivor) while polling a trivial unrelated endpoint (/api/auth/verify/) every ~1s -- all probes returned in 100-200ms throughout the burst (previously: total server freeze, confirmed via a 15s+ timeout on the same trivial endpoint and CPU-pegged R-state process before the fix). Confirmed get_series_info's actual response is unchanged (Survivor: 50 seasons, 719 episodes, matching pre-fix behavior) -- this was a performance/concurrency fix, not a behavior change. Also confirmed catalog listing (15 categories) and stream playback (206 partial content) still work end-to-end after the refactor.

title: Settings modal has vestigial legacy Dispatcharr URL/token + Disconnect button
description: Discovered live 2026-07-21: user asked why the gear-icon Settings modal still has a single Dispatcharr URL + API Token connection form and a Disconnect from Dispatcharr button, given the app has long since moved to a multi-instance Dispatcharr Connections model (managed in the main page). Both were leftover from before that architecture existed -- the single URL/token pair gated the ENTIRE app behind require_configured (is_configured() checking that one legacy config value), and Disconnect just cleared that same value, re-triggering the gate. Neither had anything to do with the real, per-connection Dispatcharr Connections a user actually manages today. User also asked where Dispatcharr Connections should live architecturally -- confirmed it should stay in the main page (same place as API Keys), not be duplicated into the account-level Settings modal.
close_reason: Removed the legacy Dispatcharr URL/token form and Disconnect button from Settings.tsx entirely -- it's now purely account-level (set up/change login credentials, nothing else). Changed app gating: first-run now requires setting login credentials (with a 'Skip for now' option, since running without a login was already a supported state), not connecting Dispatcharr -- since the app is fully usable (add providers, curate a pool, serve it via Connected Instances) without ever configuring a Dispatcharr connection at all. Backend: removed require_configured entirely from routes.py (was 503'ing every /api/vod/* and /api/backup/* route until the legacy Dispatcharr config existed), simplified GET /api/settings/ to just {has_credentials}, removed the now-dead POST /settings/, /settings/test/, /settings/disconnect/ endpoints. Dispatcharr Connections stays exactly where it already lived -- in the main page, alongside Connected Instances and API Keys -- confirmed live via browser: gear-icon modal now shows only login credentials with a Back link, main page's Dispatcharr Connections card unchanged and unaffected. Verified an existing logged-in session continued working unaffected by the change (no forced re-auth), and a real vod_routes call (GET /api/vod/providers/) succeeds without any Dispatcharr-configured precondition.

title: Manual Dispatcharr Connection add gives no feedback when vod_relay_account_id is unset
description: Discovered live 2026-07-20: user added dispatch2 via the 'Manual / existing connections' form (label/URL/token only) instead of the 'Connect a new instance (automated)' panel. The manual form just stores the row -- it never creates the Dispatcharr-side M3U account or sets vod_relay_account_id, so sync_provider() silently skips that connection (it only syncs to connections with vod_relay_account_id set) and every provider Synced count stays unchanged. No error, no warning, no indication in the UI that anything is missing or what to do about it. Fix: either (a) make vod_relay_account_id editable inline in the manual connections table so a user who already has an M3U account can wire it up after the fact, and/or (b) show a clear inline warning (not receiving syncs, no VOD-relay account set) on any connection row with a null vod_relay_account_id, and/or (c) add a Test connection action that actually calls the Dispatcharr API with the stored token and reports success/failure immediately instead of the row silently doing nothing until someone notices providers are not syncing.
close_reason: The inline-editable vod_relay_account_id field (fix option a) already existed in the manual connections table -- the actual gap was purely visual (option b): nothing distinguished 'not yet configured' from 'configured', so a user had no way to notice a connection was silently doing nothing. Added a clear inline warning (AlertCircle + 'not syncing' text, full explanation in the tooltip) that appears whenever vod_relay_account_id is null, directly next to the editable field. Verified live: created a throwaway test connection with no relay account, confirmed the warning renders with the correct explanatory tooltip, then deleted the test connection. Option (c), a dedicated 'Test connection' action, was intentionally not built -- the warning plus the already-existing editable field fully addresses the reported problem (silent no-op with zero feedback); a connectivity-test button would be a nice-to-have, not required to close this gap.

title: VOD server-side transcoding for browser-incompatible files
description:
The in-app VOD test player (native HTML5) can only play browser-native formats (MP4/H.264/AAC, WebM). Verified via direct server log correlation that many real IPTV files (.avi containers, MKV with DTS/AC-3 audio or XviD/DivX video) fail purely due to browser codec limitations -- the relay itself works correctly (confirmed 206 Partial Content + full byte transfer with zero errors on a failing file). Need server-side on-the-fly transcoding to a browser-compatible format for the in-app player specifically (external players like VLC already handle these files fine via Copy URL, so this only matters for in-app playback).

Scope questions to resolve: (1) transcode whole-file to MP4 vs live HLS segmenting for seek support, (2) on-demand (start when Play is clicked) vs pre-transcode, (3) does this apply only to the in-app player URL path (movie-source/series-source preview + normal preview) or also to the real public XC catalog stream endpoints.

STATUS AT MIGRATION (2026-07-19): was in_progress at time of extraction into the standalone VOD Manager project. See follow-up epgmatcharr-9zp.24 (migrated as a separate issue) for the known-remaining seek-support gap in the initial implementation.
close_reason: Built and verified live: _transcode_vod_stream in xc_server.py re-encodes to H.264/AAC on the fly for movie-source/series-source preview paths, confirmed working against a real Plex-sourced .avi file. Whole-file transcode (not HLS), on-demand (starts when Play is clicked, not pre-transcoded), scoped to the in-app preview endpoints only, not the public XC catalog stream endpoints (matches the original scope questions in this issue). Real seek support (HLS segmenting) remains open as vod_manager-1qk -- a jump-to-timestamp partial fix (fresh stream at a chosen offset via ffmpeg -ss, not mid-stream scrubbing) shipped instead as a cheaper stopgap for the actual workflow need (skip past an intro to verify a title).

This issue is being kept open only to track any remaining follow-up/polish beyond that initial working integration (e.g. richer library-selection UI, transcode-profile tuning) -- re-scope or close if the base integration is considered fully done.

title: No connection-limit coordination between downstream live-TV accounts and VOD provider for the same real upstream
description:
Discovered 2026-07-18 (originally in EPGmatcharr, before VOD Manager was extracted into this standalone project) while adding a live-TV-style provider as a VOD source: it's common for the same real upstream IPTV provider to already have a native live-TV account/connection elsewhere (e.g. in Dispatcharr) that VOD Manager has zero visibility into. VOD Manager connects to that same real provider completely independently for VOD purposes, tracked under its own separate synthetic account purely to track VOD Manager's own traffic in the downstream system's connection accounting.

Risk: if the real upstream provider has a hard connection cap (most XC providers do), live TV + VOD traffic can combine and exceed it even though each side's own max_streams looks fine in isolation, since neither system subtracts the other's active connections.

Current mitigation: none automated. User should manually set the VOD provider's max_streams conservatively (real provider's total limit minus live TV's typical usage) as a soft workaround.

Acceptance criteria: not yet scoped -- would need either (a) real-time cross-system connection accounting (query the other system's live viewer count for the matching native account before allowing a new VOD stream), or (b) a documented manual-budgeting workflow as the permanent answer if (a) is judged not worth the complexity.
close_reason: Fully resolved. The single-instance coordination mechanism (_has_capacity/_live_viewer_count) turned out to already exist in the code but was unconfigured for any real provider -- fixed by configuring it for all 6 real IPTV providers with their actual subscription limits (4KLive 18, Infinity 20, FTV 5, Mega-OTT 7, Lumen 4, Pink 6). The multi-instance gap (only checking one hardcoded Dispatcharr account) was the same root cause as vod_manager-e2m -- both fixed together via the new dispatcharr_connections/provider_live_accounts model: _has_capacity now sums live viewer counts across every linked live account on every connected Dispatcharr instance before comparing to a provider's real total limit. Verified live: a real stream request against Infinity correctly queried its linked live-TV account's viewer count before proceeding.

title: Per-component backup/restore/reset UI
description: Backend (backup.py: list/download/restore/reset per component) + frontend (Backup & Restore SectionCard in VodManager.tsx) allowing independent backup/restore/reset of config.json, sessions.json, and vod_db.sqlite -- e.g. wipe a corrupt database without touching credentials. SQLite downloads use VACUUM INTO for a safe consistent snapshot. Restore/reset always stash the previous file to DATA_DIR/backups/ rather than deleting. Verified end-to-end: download, reset-to-fresh (schema re-init works), and restore-from-backup all confirmed working live.
close_reason: Implemented and verified working end-to-end (download/restore/reset for all 3 components, live-tested against real data).

title: Providers table overflows its containing card width
description: The Providers section table (name/base_url/movie/series/episode counts/priority/max streams/dispatcharr profile/live-acct-limit/user-agent-override/actions) is wider than the SectionCard/page frame it's rendered in, causing bad horizontal overflow. Needs responsive handling -- likely a horizontal-scroll container scoped to the table itself (matching the artifact/page convention of 'wide content scrolls in its own container, page body never scrolls horizontally'), and/or trimming which columns show by default vs. behind an expand.
close_reason: Wrapped the Providers table in an overflow-x-auto container with a min-w-[1100px] on the table itself, matching the established 'wide content scrolls in its own container, page body never scrolls horizontally' convention already used elsewhere. Verified live via browser screenshot: table now shows its own horizontal scrollbar and stays fully contained within the SectionCard boundary instead of blowing out the page layout.

title: Page layout doesn't use available window width well at different resolutions
description: The main content area (currently a fixed max-w-5xl wrapper around all SectionCards) doesn't adapt well across different window sizes/resolutions -- framed content areas are narrower than the available window, wasting space on larger screens, per user observation 2026-07-20. Needs a proper responsive width strategy rather than one fixed max-width.
close_reason: Replaced the fixed max-w-5xl wrapper with a responsive max-w-5xl xl:max-w-6xl 2xl:max-w-7xl mx-auto -- widens progressively on larger screens (capped, not unbounded, since some sections are prose/forms that would look bad stretched to full width) and actually centers the content (previously pinned to the left edge inside its full-width parent, wasting all the slack space on the right specifically). Verified live via browser at a 2400px viewport: content now uses noticeably more width and is properly centered with equal margins, versus the old narrow left-pinned layout.

title: VOD Settings: stale single-Dispatcharr-account reference, now redundant with Connected Instances
description:
The 'VOD Settings' section still shows a field for 'the Dispatcharr M3U account (type XC) pointing back at VOD Manager's own catalog server' (xc_account_id) -- this predates the 2026-07-19/20 multi-instance auth work (per-instance XC clients, Connected Instances panel). That single-account field doesn't really apply anymore now that VOD Manager supports multiple independent Dispatcharr instances via Connected Instances below it, and its continued presence is confusing (unclear to a user which one 'wins' or what it's even for now).

Note: xc_account_id / vod_xc_account_id still has a real, separate purpose -- vod_sync.py uses it to push each provider's max_streams into ONE specific Dispatcharr instance's connection-limit profiles (see vod_manager-e2m, which already tracks generalizing that to all connected instances). So this field can't just be deleted outright; it needs either: (a) clearer labeling explaining what it's actually for now (the profile-sync target, not 'the' XC account), or (b) removal/hiding pending vod_manager-e2m's resolution if that ends up eliminating the need for a single designated account entirely.
close_reason: Confirmed vestigial: vod_sync.py's provider-limit push was fully generalized to all Dispatcharr Connections back in vod_manager-e2m, and grepped every remaining usage of get_vod_xc_account_id/save_vod_xc_account_id -- only the already-executed one-time migration (_migrate_primary_dispatcharr_connection) still references it, nothing user-facing does. Removed the xc_account_id field and its intro paragraph from the frontend, removed the now-unused /api/vod/settings/ GET/POST route, VodSettingsRequest model, and their imports from vod_routes.py. Renamed the SectionCard from 'VOD Settings' to 'API Keys' since that's now its accurate, sole purpose (TMDB + Anthropic keys). Left config.py's get/save_vod_xc_account_id functions in place since the one-time migration still calls them on fresh installs -- pure internal plumbing, not user-facing, no reason to touch. Verified live: tsc clean, browser-confirmed the card renders correctly with no trace of the stale field.

title: AI-vision-assisted auto-validation for ambiguous year-review matches
description:
Follow-up to the year-review/duplicate-reconciliation system (needs_year_review flag + TMDB search suggestions, built 2026-07-20): for users who want full automation instead of manually picking a TMDB suggestion for each flagged item, use an AI vision API to compare the flagged item's own poster/thumbnail (if the provider's raw catalog listing includes one -- check vod_importer.py's import functions for whether poster_url gets captured pre-enrichment, since flagged items are by definition often unenriched) against each TMDB candidate's poster, and auto-resolve when confident.

Needs real design before building, not a quick add-on:
- Which AI/vision provider, and the cost/rate-limit implications of calling it per flagged item (could be thousands of items in a backlog).
- Confidence threshold for auto-resolving without a human vs. falling back to the existing manual-suggestion-picker UI when uncertain.
- Fallback behavior when the flagged item has no usable source image at all (very possible pre-enrichment).
- Should this run automatically in the background (e.g. as part of the enrichment scheduler) or only on-demand/opt-in per user action.

Depends on the core year-review system already being in place (needs_year_review column, TMDB search suggestions, resolve endpoint) since this is meant to feed into that same resolve flow, just automating the picking step.
close_reason: Folded into the broader AI-assist feature built 2026-07-20 (Anthropic API integration), per explicit user direction to fold this in alongside AI-assisted smart categories. Implemented as text-based reasoning (item name + TMDB candidates' overview/cast/season-episode counts), not the originally-scoped vision/poster comparison -- a deliberate scope narrowing, agreed with the user, to avoid the unresolved design questions the issue itself flagged (vision provider choice, per-item cost/rate-limits across a large backlog, whether poster_url is even captured pre-enrichment). Also deliberately narrower on autonomy: this always returns a suggestion (best_match_index + reasoning + confidence) for the reviewer to weigh, never auto-resolves -- resolve/ remains a separate, explicit human action. New route: GET /needs-review/{content_type}/{item_id}/ai-suggest/, backed by ai_assist.suggest_year_review_match(), surfaced as an 'Ask AI' button in the NeedsReviewRow UI. Verified live with a real Anthropic API key: correctly picked between the 2005 animated and 2024 live-action 'Avatar: The Last Airbender' with sound reasoning and an honest 'medium' confidence rating. If true vision-based auto-resolve is still wanted later, this closes as superseded-by-simpler-solution, not as fully replacing that original idea -- a fresh issue would be the right way to pick that back up.

title: vod_sync.py provider-limit push only targets one Dispatcharr instance
description:
vod_sync.py's sync_provider() pushes each VOD provider's max_streams into Dispatcharr M3U account profiles for real connection-limit enforcement, but it's hardcoded to ONE Dispatcharr account (config.get_vod_xc_account_id()) -- the same single-instance assumption the 2026-07-19 auth work (vod_manager-... per-instance XC clients) just removed from the auth path.

Now that multiple independent Dispatcharr instances (3 prod behind Gluetun + 1 test behind Starlink CGNAT) can pull from the same VOD Manager pool via distinct xc_clients, this push only reaches whichever ONE instance is configured as vod_xc_account_id -- the other instances' Dispatcharr-side connection accounting for these providers is never updated.

Generalizing this requires VOD Manager to know how to reach multiple Dispatcharr APIs (URL + token per instance), not just one -- real config/UI work, deliberately deferred rather than folded into the auth change. Revisit once it's clear whether per-provider stream caps actually need enforcing identically across all connected instances, or whether a single primary instance's accounting is sufficient in practice.
close_reason: Fixed together with vod_manager-hgl (same root cause: VOD Manager assumed exactly one Dispatcharr instance). New dispatcharr_connections table generalizes the single implicit connection into a real list; vod_sync.sync_provider now pushes each provider's max_streams to every connection with a vod_relay_account_id configured, not just one. Each connection gets its own separate Dispatcharr profile object (provider_sync_profiles, per-connection since profile ids aren't shared across separate Dispatcharr databases). Also added connect_dispatcharr_instance to automate adding a new instance end-to-end (creates the XC client + Dispatcharr-side M3U account + saved connection in one call) instead of the manual multi-step process this previously required. Verified live: migration correctly carried over the existing connection, all 8 providers sync cleanly to it.

title: XC exposure hardening: deployment guidance + configurable lockout
description:
Follow-up to the 2026-07-19 XC auth hardening pass (timing-safe comparison, credential log redaction, per-IP lockout after 10 failed attempts/5min -> 15min lockout, all in xc_server.py). Remaining items, in case this server is ever reachable beyond a trusted LAN/VPN:

1. Deployment guidance (docs, not code): recommend never exposing the XC port directly to the public internet without TLS in front of it (reverse proxy or VPN/tunnel) -- the app itself doesn't enforce TLS. Document this clearly in README/USERGUIDE once those exist for this project.
2. Lockout thresholds (10 attempts/5min, 15min lockout) are currently hardcoded constants in xc_server.py -- consider exposing via Settings if real-world usage shows they need tuning (e.g. shared-NAT households hitting false-positive lockouts).
3. No visibility/admin UI for currently-locked-out IPs -- an operator can only see lockouts via container logs (WARNING level). Low priority unless it becomes a real support pain point.
4. Lockout state is in-memory/per-process -- resets on every container restart. Acceptable given the threat model (slows down sustained automated brute-force; a restart-and-retry attacker is a much smaller threat), but worth knowing if it ever needs to survive restarts.
close_reason: Done: (1) README.md now documents TLS/reverse-proxy/VPN deployment guidance under Security and deployment. (2) Lockout thresholds (max attempts, window, duration) are now configurable via Settings -> Security, backed by config.py's get/save_lockout_settings() and a new GET/POST /api/vod/lockout-settings/ route; xc_server.py reads them through a 30s-cached _lockout_settings() instead of hardcoded constants. Verified live: lowered threshold to 3 attempts, confirmed a real lockout log line and a 4th request rejected as locked out, then restored defaults. (3) Admin visibility for locked-out IPs left as-is (container logs only) -- issue itself flagged this low priority. (4) In-memory/per-process lockout state left as-is -- issue itself judged this an acceptable trade-off.

title: Configurable refresh intervals for VOD providers/TMDB sync
description:
Background refresh scheduling in main.py is hardcoded and undifferentiated: the catalog refresher runs ALL provider types (XC, Plex, Emby, Jellyfin) on one shared 6-hour interval, the enrichment scheduler runs on a separate hardcoded 24h interval, and TMDB category sync has no scheduler at all (manual 'Sync now' button only). None of these are exposed as user-configurable settings.

Motivated by discovering a single Plex library refresh can take 18+ minutes and generate gigabytes of disk I/O (root cause: missing index on episodes(series_id, season_number, episode_number), fixed separately) -- as Emby/Jellyfin join Plex as 'big library' providers alongside XC's cheap catalog pulls, a one-size-fits-all interval stops making sense. Needs: per-provider-type (or per-provider) configurable catalog refresh interval, configurable enrichment interval, and optionally a TMDB auto-sync interval, exposed via config.py + a VOD Manager settings UI section.
close_reason: Built: per-provider-type configurable catalog refresh interval (XC/Plex/Emby/Jellyfin each independently tunable, addressing the original 18+ min Plex-scan-on-shared-6h-interval motivation), configurable enrichment TTL, and a new opt-in TMDB Lists auto-sync scheduler (previously manual-only). Backend: config.py get/save_refresh_settings(), vod_db.py new providers.last_catalog_refresh_at column + cached _refresh_settings()/get_enrichment_ttl_seconds()/get_catalog_refresh_interval_seconds()/mark_provider_catalog_refreshed(), main.py's _vod_catalog_refresher rewritten to poll every 5min and refresh only providers actually due per their type's interval (was: one shared 6h sleep for everything), new _tmdb_sync_scheduler task (disabled by default). API: GET/POST /api/vod/refresh-settings/. Frontend: new 'Refresh Schedule' Settings card (hours-based inputs, seconds under the hood). Verified live: py_compile + tsc clean, Docker rebuild, migration confirmed (last_catalog_refresh_at column present, NULL on existing rows), first-boot correctly flagged all 8 active providers as due (matching old always-refresh-on-fresh-state behavior), confirmed a provider (4KLive) got stamped with a real timestamp after its refresh completed, POST/GET round-tripped custom values, browser-verified the Settings UI renders and reflects saved values, then restored sane defaults (6h all types, 24h enrichment, TMDB sync off).

title: HLS segmented transcoding for VOD in-app player (real seek support)
description:
Follow-up to the base VOD server-side transcoding feature. Current transcode implementation pipes a live fragmented-MP4 (empty_moov) from ffmpeg directly to the browser -- works for playback but has no duration/seek support since the browser has no upfront length (behaves like a 'live' stream, growing duration = however much has streamed so far, forward-play only). Real fix: ffmpeg outputs HLS segments (.ts + .m3u8 playlist) instead, giving browsers native seek support. Bigger scope than the base transcode feature -- needs segment file management (temp dir per session), playlist generation, segment cleanup/expiry on stream close, and handling of concurrent transcode sessions without exhausting disk/CPU.

Deliberately deferred -- current 'plays but can't seek' behavior is acceptable for now since this is an in-app *test* player; real end viewers use external players via Dispatcharr and never hit this transcode path at all.
close_reason: Built the real fix described in the issue: ffmpeg now outputs a proper HLS playlist + .ts segments for the in-app test player instead of a single forward-only fMP4 pipe, giving genuine seek support. Backend (xc_server.py): per-session temp dir (tempfile.mkdtemp), ffmpeg run with -f hls -hls_playlist_type event -hls_list_size 0 (keeps all segments so backward seek works across everything encoded so far) and -hls_base_url embedding the client's own credentials + a random 128-bit session id, new routes /preview/{movie,series}-source-hls/.../index.m3u8 and /hls-segment/{username}/{password}/{hls_id}/{segment}, both re-validating auth AND the per-instance category allowlist from vod_manager-4zj (same access control now covers this path too). Session lifecycle: registered in the existing Activity panel (title suffixed '(HLS)'), Kill button now tears down HLS sessions directly (kill_session detects _hls_id and calls _stop_hls_session, since there's no single long-lived request loop to flag like the other stream kinds have -- ffmpeg writes segments independently of whether anyone's fetching them). Idle sessions (45s with no playlist/segment request) are torn down automatically by a new standalone hls_sweep_loop background task registered in main.py's lifespan -- not just an opportunistic check inside the request handlers, which would silently leak a running ffmpeg process and its temp directory forever if no further HLS traffic ever happened again (caught this exact gap live during testing: an opportunistic-only sweep left a session running 90+ seconds past its timeout with zero other HLS traffic; fixed by adding the dedicated background loop). Frontend: added hls.js dependency, VodPlayer gained a third playback mode alongside direct/transcoded with its own useEffect-managed Hls.js instance (falls back to native <video> src for Safari), wired into all 6 existing PlayButton call sites (Needs Review, movie list, movie source rows, series episode rows) alongside the existing transcode fallback -- offered as a separate button since HLS's slower start is a real tradeoff against the existing fast-start forward-only transcode, not a strict replacement. Verified live end-to-end against a real movie: playlist generation (six-plus seconds for ffmpeg to produce a real segment, confirmed valid MPEG-TS via ), segment fetch, Activity panel visibility, Kill-button teardown (confirmed via docker exec /proc scan: zero lingering ffmpeg processes, temp dir removed, segment 404s after kill), and the standalone idle-timeout sweep (confirmed a session with zero further requests was torn down automatically ~45-60s later with no manual kill, after first misdiagnosing this as broken because my own verification curls to the segment URL were themselves resetting the idle timer -- corrected by checking via docker exec/logs only). tsc + py_compile clean throughout, Docker rebuild clean. README.md documents the feature and its real tradeoffs (slower start, backward-seek-only-so-far, real CPU/disk cost while active).

title: Integrate external watchlist/playlist sources (TMDB Lists, Trakt, etc.) as syncing categories
description:
Idea raised 2026-07-18 (originally in EPGmatcharr, before VOD Manager was extracted into this standalone project): let users create playlists sourced from external services (Trakt watchlists/lists, possibly others) that auto-populate a VOD category, visible to all users (consistent with the finding that Dispatcharr has no per-user VOD split -- see the per-profile access control backlog item -- so 'shared for everyone' is the natural fit, not a limitation here).

Worth noting: this reuses most infrastructure already built, rather than being a from-scratch feature:
- Categories already exist as the placement mechanism (movie_category_placements/series_category_placements).
- movies.tmdb_id / imdb_id are already captured during enrichment (from the provider's own TMDB-quality get_vod_info data) -- Trakt's API also deals in tmdb_id/imdb_id, so matching a Trakt list entry to our pool could be done reliably by ID rather than fuzzy name+year matching.
- The scheduled background task pattern (_vod_catalog_refresher, _vod_enrichment_scheduler in main.py) is a direct template for a periodic '_trakt_sync' task.
- bulk_place_movies_in_category/bulk_place_series_in_category are exactly the right primitive for bulk-placing a whole synced list at once.

New work needed: Trakt API client (OAuth or API key depending on which Trakt endpoints are used -- public lists vs personal watchlists have different auth requirements), a 'sync_source' concept on categories (e.g. trakt list URL/ID stored alongside rule_json), UI for configuring a category's external source, id-based matching logic (tmdb_id primary, imdb_id fallback, name+year last resort for pool items never enriched).

---
NOTES:
Research 2026-07-18 -- viable free API options, ranked:
1. TMDB Lists (STRONGEST candidate) -- we already capture tmdb_id for nearly every enriched movie/series, so matching a TMDB list's entries to our pool is exact ID-to-ID, no fuzzy name/year logic needed. Free API key, no OAuth required to read a public list. Lowest-effort integration by far given what's already built.
2. Trakt -- free tier, well-documented, the de facto standard in the self-hosted media world (Radarr/Sonarr/Overseerr all support it). More setup (OAuth for personal watchlists) but richer feature set if that ever matters.
3. Simkl -- lesser-known Trakt alternative, free public API, movies/TV/anime.
4. MyAnimeList -- free public list API, worth considering given the pool has a real 'Anime' category already.

Ruled out: Letterboxd (no public third-party list API despite popularity), IMDb (no usable public list API; imdb_id still useful as a matching key since we already store it, just not as a list source).

Recommendation when picked up: start with TMDB Lists specifically -- it sidesteps the hardest part (reliable matching) almost entirely.

Acceptance criteria: not yet scoped -- design/research item for when picked up. Should evaluate Trakt API auth model (public list API keys vs OAuth) before implementation.
close_reason: Already fully built and confirmed working in production: TMDB Lists integration (config.py TMDB key, tmdb_sync.py fetch_list_items/sync_category/sync_all with exact tmdb_id matching, vod_routes.py sync-source/sync-now endpoints, VodManager.tsx 'TMDB Lists' card with Add-list form and per-category manual sync). This was the issue's own top-ranked recommendation ('start with TMDB Lists specifically -- sidesteps the hardest part'). Verified live against the user's real 'Steven's Movie Picks' list (TMDB list 8671792) via browser click -> Sync now -> log confirms match against the real pool. Corrected a stale docstring in tmdb_sync.py claiming a scheduled task already calls sync_all() (it doesn't yet -- tracked separately as vod_manager-tq3). Trakt/Simkl/MyAnimeList integrations from the original title were research options, not requirements -- not pursued since TMDB Lists alone satisfies the use case; can reopen a fresh issue if a non-TMDB source is specifically wanted later.

title: Per-profile VOD access control via direct XC server exposure
description:
Confirmed 2026-07-17 (originally in EPGmatcharr, before VOD Manager was extracted into this standalone project): Dispatcharr has NO per-user/per-profile VOD filtering anywhere in its own output paths -- channel_profile only appears on the HDHomeRun (live-TV tuning) output endpoints, nothing VOD-equivalent exists. Once a movie/series is imported into Dispatcharr's shared Movie/Series tables, every Dispatcharr user sees the identical catalog; there's no split.

For real per-user/profile VOD control (the IPTVBoss-style experience wanted), the only viable path is bypassing Dispatcharr's VOD pass-through entirely for those users: expose our own xc_server directly to end-user IPTV apps (TiviMate, IPTV Smarters, etc.) with multiple distinct credential sets, each mapped to a different allowed-category list on our side. Would need: multi-credential auth in xc_server.py (currently validates against a single shared Dispatcharr-account username/password), a per-credential category-visibility mapping, and a decision on whether/how this is exposed beyond the local network.

Deliberately parked 2026-07-17 -- revisit later.
close_reason: Built the recommended path from the issue's own design: multi-credential auth already existed (xc_clients table); added a per-credential category-visibility allowlist (xc_clients.category_allowlist, comma-separated category ids, NULL = unrestricted so every existing client is unaffected). Enforced at every layer, not just catalog hiding: get_vod_categories/get_vod_streams/get_series_categories/get_series filtered, get_vod_info/get_series_info return empty for disallowed items, and all 8 streaming routes (movie/series placed-content play, preview by movie/episode id, preview by specific source, both transcoded variants) independently verify category membership before proxying -- so a restricted client can't reach disallowed content even via a direct/copied stream URL, not just have it hidden from browse. New vod_db.py helpers: get_movie_category_ids/get_series_category_ids, update_xc_client gained category_allowlist/clear_category_allowlist params. API: PATCH /api/vod/clients/{id}/ extended. Frontend: new 'Category access' column in Connected Instances with an expandable movie/TV checkbox picker (Save/Clear-allow-all/Cancel), Save disabled on an empty selection to prevent the empty-string-means-unrestricted footgun. Verified live end-to-end: created a real test client restricted to one category, confirmed get_vod_categories/get_series_categories/get_vod_streams/get_series all correctly filtered (1208 streams, all category 14), confirmed get_vod_info returns {} for a disallowed movie, confirmed the actual /movie/ stream route returns 206 for an allowed movie and 404 for a disallowed one, confirmed the preview/movie/ route (movie_id-based) also 404s for disallowed content, confirmed the existing unrestricted Dispatcharr-instance clients are completely unaffected (still 15 categories, still 206 on the same disallowed-for-restricted stream), then deleted the test client. README.md documents the feature. Left as noted in the original issue: whether/how this gets exposed beyond the local network is a deployment decision (same TLS/tunnel guidance as the rest of the XC exposure), not a new mechanism -- no additional work needed there.

title: Explore ingesting Dispatcharr DVR recordings into VOD Manager
description:
Idea raised 2026-07-17 (originally in EPGmatcharr, before VOD Manager was extracted into this standalone project): Dispatcharr has a DVR mode that records live TV to local files. If VOD Manager runs on the same machine/host as Dispatcharr (or shares a volume with it), a shared volume/bind mount could let VOD Manager ingest DVR-recorded content into the same curated pool as regular provider content.

This is a genuinely different kind of source than anything built so far -- the whole current pipeline (vod_importer.py's XCProviderClient, import_provider_catalog) assumes a remote XC provider with a JSON API that hands back rich TMDB-quality metadata (genre/cast/poster) for free. DVR recordings are just files on disk with no API and no pre-baked metadata -- only whatever Dispatcharr's DVR naming convention puts in the filename/folder structure.

Scope for whenever this gets picked up:

1. Confirm Dispatcharr's actual DVR output folder structure and filename convention (not yet investigated).
2. New 'local folder' source type (parallel to the XC provider type) that scans a directory instead of calling a remote API.
3. Filename/folder parsing for title/year (and season/episode for TV recordings) -- no upstream JSON to lean on this time.
4. Metadata gap: recordings won't come pre-enriched like the current XC provider does. Either accept blank genre/poster for DVR-sourced content, or add a real TMDB API lookup as a fallback enrichment path.

Not yet scoped to acceptance criteria -- exploratory/design item. Revisit once ready to pick this up.

title: Explore adaptive bitrate (ABR) streaming for VOD pool
description:
Idea raised during original VOD Manager build (2026-07-16): if multiple providers in the pool carry different quality renditions of the same movie (e.g. 1080p from provider A, 720p from provider B), could we expose them as a real ABR ladder instead of just failover proxying?

Current state: xc_server.py's stream proxy does straight passthrough/failover proxying between upstream sources for the same movie -- it's a reliability mechanism (try source B if source A fails), not a quality-adaptation mechanism. It swaps sources only on failure, never dynamically based on client bandwidth.

Real ABR would need either: (a) sources that are already clean, quality-labeled variants of the same title (uncommon from XC providers in practice), packaged into a proper HLS master playlist / DASH manifest so a player can switch renditions based on bandwidth, or (b) a transcode/repackage pipeline on our end to actually build the bitrate ladder ourselves -- meaningfully more infrastructure than today's proxy.

Deliberately deferred: explore only once VOD Manager's core (import, catalog export, failover, auth) is built, tested, and shipped.

---
NOTES (migrated from epgmatcharr-9zp.8, closed 2026-07-19 on extraction into standalone vod_manager project):

UPDATE 2026-07-17: found concrete evidence this isn't hypothetical. A real provider (Infinity/best-streams.tv) runs a literal '4K Movies' catalog category (2618 titles) offering '4K: ' variants of movies already in the main genre categories -- e.g. '4K: A Quiet Place Day One' alongside the plain 'A Quiet Place Day One'. Exact (name, year) matching currently treats these as two unrelated movies (different rows, different movie_id, no relationship tracked) rather than two quality renditions of the same title.

Immediate pragmatic fix applied (not the full ABR design): a dedicated '4K' smart category (rule: name starts_with '4K:') so this content is at least visibly organized as its own section rather than scattered/invisible.

Still open for real ABR design: whether/how to link '4K: X' and 'X' as the same canonical title with a quality-preference relationship (e.g. prefer 4K source when available, fall back to SD) rather than two independent catalog entries -- this is the concrete real-world case to design against when this gets picked up.
